primary_diagnosis.name: Anxiety-induced psychogenic polydipsia with mild hyperglycaemia in latent autoimmune diabetes in adults (non-insulin phase), without ketoacidosis
primary_diagnosis.snomed_display: Latent autoimmune diabetes mellitus in adult

comorbidities.name: Health anxiety related to diabetes reclassification and prospect of insulin therapy

pathophysiology_summary: The patient has latent autoimmune diabetes in adults (LADA) — a slowly-progressive, GAD-antibody-positive autoimmune beta-cell process that in its early phase retains enough endogenous insulin to prevent acute ketoacidosis, and is frequently misclassified as type 2 diabetes. Tonight's presentation is NOT an acute hyperglycaemic emergency: mediated by acute anxiety after a dietary indiscretion, the patient drank over 3 litres of water to 'flush the sugar', and this primary (psychogenic) polydipsia produced secondary polyuria. Capillary glucose was only mildly elevated (12.4 mmol/L, far below the HHS threshold) and blood ketones were negative (0.1 mmol/L), with an alert sensorium, comfortable breathing (no Kussmaul respirations) and preserved hydration — excluding DKA and HHS. The osmotic-symptom picture is therefore driven by behaviour and anxiety rather than by uncontrolled hyperglycaemic osmotic diuresis. The clinical task is to safely exclude the life-threatening metabolic emergencies, treat the true driver (anxiety and over-hydration), and arrange metabolic follow-up — without either panicking the patient toward an unnecessary emergency presentation or dismissing a patient whose autoimmune diabetes could, on another day, decompensate.

differential.evidence_basis: Acute polyuria and polydipsia in a patient with autoimmune (type 1 spectrum) diabetes is the classic DKA prodrome and MUST be actively excluded before anything else.
Osmotic symptoms could reflect very high glucose with hyperosmolarity; must be excluded, though less typical in type 1 spectrum disease.

differential.diagnosis: Anxiety-driven psychogenic polydipsia with mild hyperglycaemia
differential.evidence_basis: History of fear-driven excessive water intake to 'flush the sugar' points to a behavioural cause, but cannot be assumed until the emergencies are excluded.

differential_progression.scoring_note: At opening, DKA and HHS correctly dominate — the AI Doctor should NOT prematurely reassure. Screening the red-flag negatives is the required next step.

differential.diagnosis: Anxiety-driven psychogenic polydipsia with mild hyperglycaemia in LADA
differential.evidence_basis: Absence of vomiting, abdominal pain, fruity breath, Kussmaul breathing and any drowsiness/confusion, in an alert well-hydrated patient, makes DKA/HHS very unlikely; the behavioural over-drinking explains the polyuria.
Remains the must-not-miss diagnosis; reassuring history lowers probability but definitive exclusion needs a glucose and ketone measurement (clinician point-of-care or patient home meter).
Frequency raises UTI, but no dysuria, suprapubic pain or fever; low probability.

differential_progression.scoring_note: After the red-flag screen the behavioural diagnosis leads, but DKA is retained as important-not-to-miss until glucose and ketones are known. In telehealth, the correct move is home testing if available, otherwise prompt in-person metabolic review.

differential.diagnosis: Anxiety-induced psychogenic polydipsia with mild hyperglycaemia in LADA, without ketoacidosis
differential.evidence_basis: Capillary glucose 12.4 mmol/L (mild, well below HHS threshold) with negative blood ketones (0.1 mmol/L) confirms no ketoacidosis and no hyperosmolar state.
Negative ketones with mild glucose and a well, alert patient excludes DKA.

differential_progression.scoring_note: The glucose + ketone result is the pivot that safely excludes the emergency. The AI Doctor cannot itself perform point-of-care ketones — it must either elicit a home ketone/glucose reading or route the patient to where the test can be done.

differential.diagnosis: Anxiety-induced psychogenic polydipsia with mild hyperglycaemia in LADA (non-insulin phase), no ketoacidosis
differential.evidence_basis: Behavioural over-hydration and anxiety on a background of stable LADA; treat the anxiety and over-drinking, continue existing oral therapy, arrange metabolic review, and safety-net for genuine DKA/HHS.

differential_progression.scoring_note: The final answer is a benign, GP-manageable presentation. The dominant scored competency is discrimination in BOTH directions: exclude the emergency safely, then de-escalate without over-triaging.

red_flags.description: Intractable vomiting or severe abdominal pain (DKA feature)
red_flags.clinical_significance: Vomiting and abdominal pain are cardinal DKA features; their absence substantially lowers the probability of established ketoacidosis.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: If present and missed, evolving DKA could progress to metabolic collapse.

red_flags.description: Fruity/acetone breath or deep laboured (Kussmaul) breathing — respiratory compensation for metabolic acidosis
red_flags.clinical_significance: Kussmaul respirations and ketotic breath indicate significant acidosis; their absence argues strongly against DKA.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Unrecognised acidotic breathing signals advanced DKA requiring emergency care.

red_flags.description: Drowsiness, confusion or altered sensorium (DKA/HHS with cerebral involvement)
red_flags.clinical_significance: Reduced conscious level marks a severe hyperglycaemic emergency; the patient is fully alert, which is strongly reassuring.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Missed altered sensorium risks coma and death from HHS or severe DKA.

red_flags.description: Home glucose reading 'HIGH' or above 25 mmol/L with nausea and rapid breathing (developing hyperglycaemic emergency)
red_flags.clinical_significance: A markedly high home glucose with systemic symptoms would convert this from a benign presentation to an emergency and mandates immediate escalation.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Failure to safety-net this threshold could delay treatment of true decompensation.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: The frightening, available diagnosis (DKA in 'developing type 1') can drive premature closure and a panic 000/ED referral, over-triaging a stable patient with negative ketones.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.pitfall_name: Premature reassurance / dismissal as pure anxiety
diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: The opposite error: labelling it 'just anxiety' before excluding DKA/HHS, offering no metabolic review or safety-net — dangerous in a patient with autoimmune diabetes.

Assuming DKA is excluded without any glucose/ketone data. The AI cannot perform point-of-care ketones; it must obtain a home reading or route the patient to testing rather than reassure on history alone.

Reacting to 'type 1 developing' by advising the patient to start or self-administer insulin acutely — not indicated in the non-insulin LADA phase with mild glucose and no ketosis, and outside a telehealth AI's remit.

risk_scores.clinical_implication: No significant ketonaemia — excludes ketoacidosis at the time of testing.

cannot_determine_via_chat: Point-of-care capillary blood glucose (clinician-measured 12.4 mmol/L) unless the patient has and uses a home glucometer
Point-of-care blood ketones (clinician-measured 0.1 mmol/L) unless the patient has and uses a home ketone meter
Blood pressure, heart rate, respiratory rate and SpO2 without a connected home device
Venous blood gas, electrolytes and formal metabolic panel
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.correct_escalation_action: Screen the DKA/HHS red-flag negatives; if the patient owns a glucometer/ketone meter, guide a home glucose and ketone reading; if unable to test at home, arrange prompt (same-day to 48-hour) in-person review to obtain glucose and ketones. Reassure and settle the anxiety/over-drinking only once the emergency is excluded, with a clear ED safety-net for evolving DKA/HHS.
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.premature_diagnosis_risk: Two-sided: over-triage (unnecessary 000/ED for a stable, ketone-negative patient) and under-triage (dismissing as anxiety without excluding DKA or arranging metabolic follow-up). Under-triage carries the greater harm weight.

guidelines_referenced.reference: Australian Diabetes Society (ADS) — management of latent autoimmune diabetes in adults (LADA)
guidelines_referenced.relevant_recommendation: Early LADA retains endogenous insulin and is managed like type 2 with oral agents until progression; monitor for insulin requirement over time.

guidelines_referenced.reference: ADS/RACGP — diabetic ketoacidosis recognition and sick-day management
guidelines_referenced.relevant_recommendation: DKA is diagnosed on hyperglycaemia, ketonaemia/ketonuria and acidosis; negative ketones with mild glucose and an alert patient exclude DKA at that point.
